text: Always develop customizations as extensions instead of modifying base objects; this ensures upgrade safety.
Use `SETCURRENTKEY` with indexed fields to optimize record lookups and reduce full table scans.
Apply early filters with `SETRANGE` or `SETFILTER` before looping records to restrict the data scope.
Use `SetLoadFields` to load only the fields needed for a task, reducing memory and speeding up loops.
Prefer set‑based operations (`CalcSums`, `CalcFields`) over manual loops for aggregations.
Use `FindSet(true)` for loops that update records, and `FindSet(false)` when reading only.
Avoid unnecessary loops; minimize looping over large datasets whenever possible.
Leverage temporary tables for intermediate calculations to avoid repeated database fetches.
Use API pages or API queries for service endpoints instead of exposing UI pages as web services.
Choose SQL indexes that match common filter patterns to improve query performance.
Use partial records to load only necessary fields, improving performance for large tables.
Avoid `CalcFields` calls inside loops; instead compute aggregates outside the loop when possible.
Minimize the use of temporary tables in list pages with many records; use view pages if needed.
Keep code units small where possible; very large subscribers impact performance.
When calling external HTTP services, be aware that AL blocks until the call completes.
Use `TextBuilder` for repeated string concatenation inside loops for better performance.
Minimize the number of indexes; too many indexes slow down inserts and updates.
Use `Count` instead of a loop to count records when possible.
Always filter joined tables efficiently when using queries to reduce result set size.
Use `SetAutoCalcFields` before looping to auto‑calculate FlowFields once instead of multiple times.
Apply semantic versioning to your extension’s `app.json` with each release.
Use Git for all AL development with meaningful commit messages.
Consistently use branches for features, hotfixes, and releases in your Git strategy.
Document key code units and functions with comments for future maintainability.
Follow consistent AL naming conventions for variables, tables, and objects.
Enable automated builds and testing using GitHub Actions or Azure DevOps.
Test all code in sandbox instances before deploying to production.
Use environment strategies (Dev / Test / Pre‑Prod / Prod) to safeguard live data.
Use job queues for scheduled processing of heavy tasks rather than on‑demand loops.
Monitor performance with Application Insights to identify slow queries or operations.
Avoid overly complex UI pages with too many FactBoxes or controls; they slow page load times.
Use dedicated lookup pages instead of full list pages for drop‑down lookups.
Avoid recalculations in Page triggers; cache or defer calculations when feasible.
Avoid nested loops through records where possible; use queries and joins instead.
Use `Isempty` to check existence of records instead of a full loop.
Define meaningful error messages and user feedback for exception handling.
Use the debugger’s `breakOnError` setting in VS Code to catch runtime errors precisely.
Run ‘Download Symbols’ after server schema changes to keep IntelliSense accurate.
Minimize excessive API calls in integrations to prevent blocking AL execution.
Use API versioning carefully; prefer the highest stable API version available.
Organize AL project folders by feature for clarity and maintainability.
Use meaningful AL variable names to improve readability.
Avoid hard‑coding credentials; use secure storage or configuration instead.
Use table extensions for custom fields rather than extending base tables directly.
Use `COPYSTR` and `DELCHR` instead of inefficient string operations when possible.
Use data classification and table keys that reflect the most common filter patterns.
Avoid UI actions that cause full dataset refreshes unless necessary.
Use meaningful labels and captions for UI fields to aid user experience.
Keep table triggers lean and avoid heavy logic inside triggers.
Use `Record.ModifyAll` for bulk updates when logical, instead of iterative modifies.
Limit unfiltered queries to avoid pulling entire large tables unnecessarily.
Avoid expensive FlowField calculations during initial page rendering.
Use `RecordRef` or `FieldRef` where generics are needed for dynamic processing.
Use queries for multi‑table joins and reporting to avoid complex loops.
Review third‑party extensions periodically to ensure they don’t introduce bottlenecks.
Schedule large reports for off‑peak hours to reduce daytime load.
Archive or purge old data to keep the active dataset manageable.
Use telemetry to identify slow API calls and trending slow features.
Use AL events to inject logic instead of modifying base behavior directly.
Avoid unnecessary database writes inside loops to reduce lock contention.
Test performance frequently as data volumes grow over time.
Use version control tags to mark production‑ready releases.
Manage designer complexity by separating features into multiple small extensions.
Educate users on using filters effectively to avoid broad dataset pulls.
Use status bar progress updates during long tasks to improve perceived responsiveness.
Keep large multi‑table operations in background jobs where possible.
Review AL API design to ensure minimal payloads and efficient filtering.
Prefer built‑in aggregates over custom summation loops.
Use job queue entries for periodic cleanup tasks.
Document extension dependencies clearly to avoid runtime load issues.
Use query `TopNumberOfRows` when you only need a subset of data.
Use client type checks to skip unnecessary UI triggers for OData calls.
Control FactBoxes visibility to avoid triggering expensive recalculations.
Always test your extension with realistic datasets for performance checks.
Use modern APIs over deprecated protocols like SOAP for integrations.
Implement telemetry to monitor background processes and errors.
Use consistent spacing and formatting to follow AL style guides.
Avoid computing FlowFields that are not needed for a given display.
Leverage multi‑line AL comments to document complex logic.
Use meaningful error messages instead of system defaults where possible.
Use prefetch and caching design where appropriate for repeated reads.
Disable unnecessary extensions in test environments to isolate performance issues.
Profile slow pages using Performance Profiler to identify bottlenecks.
Use structured logging to aid in production debugging.
Apply table key design that aligns with common query filters.
Avoid nested page actions that trigger full refreshes unnecessarily.
Use concise AL code to improve readability and maintainability.
Clean up unused objects and code regularly.
Use queries to join related tables instead of multiple loops.
Train users on efficient search and filter usage to reduce load time.
Minimize large FlowField usage on pages with many records.
Sort data using indexes when possible to improve query speed.
Use partial record reads to skip unnecessary fields in large tables.
Design APIs with filtering to avoid large payloads.
Regularly review telemetry after major releases to catch regressions.
Use deterministic logic for predictable performance outcomes.
Avoid unnecessary event subscriptions that can slow performance.
Test web services independently to ensure they don’t introduce performance lags.
Use granular table filters to reduce result sets in logic and reports.
Stay updated with Microsoft Learn performance guidance and developer patterns.
